title: Phirni

ingredients.name: raw rice, washed
visual_checks: soak in water for 30 minutes; the soaked grains grind to a better paste
ingredients.notes: soak in water for 30 minutes; the soaked grains grind to a better paste

subs.name: 1 tbsp cornflour (cornstarch) dissolved in 3 tbsp cold milk — the texture is smoother and sets faster, but lacks the grit of traditional phirni
full-fat canned coconut milk thinned with water (1:1) — dairy-free with a distinctly different but pleasing flavour
kewra (pandanus) essence, 2–3 drops — a different floral note equally traditional in North Indian sweets

steps.text: Drain the soaked rice and grind in a mixie with 3 tbsp of the milk to a coarse-gritty paste — aim for the texture of very fine wet sand, not a smooth slurry. This grainy texture is what defines phirni and distinguishes it from plain kheer, where the rice cooks whole.
Pour the remaining milk into a wide, heavy-bottomed saucepan and heat over medium flame, stirring frequently. Bring to a full boil, then reduce heat to medium-low.

Pour the ground rice paste in a thin stream into the boiling milk, stirring constantly and vigorously to prevent lumps. Stir every minute for 20–25 minutes as the phirni slowly thickens — it will progress from milky liquid to a thin porridge to a creamy, gently thickened pudding that coats the back of a spoon and holds a trail from a spoon drawn through it.

Add sugar and stir for 3–4 more minutes until dissolved and the phirni tightens back slightly. Remove from heat. Stir in saffron milk, cardamom and rose water. Taste and adjust sugar. The phirni will thicken further as it cools and sets — aim for a slightly runnier consistency than what you want to serve.

Pour into individual earthen kulhads (clay cups) or small bowls. Cool to room temperature for 30 minutes, then cover with cling film and refrigerate for at least 2 hours or overnight until the phirni is set to a thick, soft-pudding consistency. Garnish with slivered pistachios and almonds immediately before serving. Serve cold.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.

trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: rice → 1 tbsp cornflour (cornstarch) dissolved in 3 tbsp cold milk — the texture is smoother and sets faster, but lacks the grit of traditional phirni; milk → full-fat canned coconut milk thinned with water (1:1) — dairy-free with a distinctly different but pleasing flavour; rose-water → kewra (pandanus) essence, 2–3 drops — a different floral note equally traditional in North Indian sweets
